context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Trade, Outsourcing, and Labor Standards

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is openness to trade directly correlated to economic growth? What does the historical record say so far? Is the increasing shift to bilateral and regional trade arrangements compatible with the possibility of international trade, and with increasing commitment to labor rights? What do we make of the economic development strategies of emerging powers like China and India in terms of trade openness and commitment to labor standards? Who monitors labor standards?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m stuck on a reflection for my development planning class about trade, outsourcing, and labor standards. I kind of understand the topics separately, but when the prompt asks me to connect trade openness, growth, bilateral/regional agreements, and labor rights, I lose the thread. Could you help me figure out what the prompt is really asking?
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point — breaking down the prompt is exactly the right move. So, when you read it carefully, how many distinct questions do you count in there? Try listing them out.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for help understanding the prompt as a whole. Rather than explaining the prompt to them, I should use the Socratic method to help them break it down themselves. I'll ask them to identify the key questions in the prompt on their own, which is a natural first step in guided discovery. This keeps the cognitive work with the student and avoids over-scaffolding.

exchanges.student: I think maybe three or four, but they all blur together for me. It feels like it’s basically asking for “everything about trade and labor,” and I’m not sure how separate I’m supposed to keep each part.
exchanges.tutor: Fair enough — but let's not let 'they blur together' stop us. Take your best shot: write out the questions you think the prompt is asking, even roughly. What's your list?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is having trouble separating the questions. Rather than listing them out for the student, I should push them to attempt the breakdown themselves. A pointed question asking them to try listing what they see, even imperfectly, will move things forward and give me something concrete to work with.

exchanges.student: Okay, my rough list is: whether trade openness leads to growth, what history shows about that, whether bilateral/regional trade deals fit with global trade and labor rights, and then what China and India show about openness versus labor standards. I’m also not sure if “who monitors labor standards” is supposed to be its own separate question or just part of the last one.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid breakdown — you've essentially identified the core questions correctly. On your uncertainty about 'who monitors': think about it this way — if labor standards exist but no one enforces them, what does that imply? Does that connect to any of your other questions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done a good job breaking down the prompt into its component questions. I should acknowledge this progress and then help them see the connecting thread between these questions, rather than answering it for them. The 'who monitors' question is a good hook to get them thinking about the underlying tension the prompt is exploring.

exchanges.student: I guess it means labor rights might be more symbolic than real, so monitoring affects whether trade agreements or growth strategies actually protect workers. That said, I still feel like I need a big picture explanation of the whole trade-growth-labor debate before I can say anything coherent.
exchanges.tutor: Good insight on monitoring — that's actually a key tension in the prompt. On needing a 'big picture explanation' first: what do your course readings say about whether trade openness reliably produces growth? Start there — what have you read so far?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student made a genuinely good connection about monitoring and enforcement. However, they are now asking for a broad explanation of the trade-growth-labor debate, which would be giving them content I shouldn't provide. Instead, I should redirect them to what they already know and push them to draw on their course readings. This keeps the cognitive work with the student and avoids over-scaffolding.

exchanges.student: Honestly I only half-get the readings, and I’m mixing them together. That didn’t really clear it up for me, so I’m trying a different angle: could you just give me a broad overview of the main positions in the debate first, and then I can try to connect them back to the readings?
exchanges.tutor: That's not something I can do — giving you an overview would mean doing the intellectual work the assignment expects from you. But you said you 'half-get' the readings. What's one thing you do remember, even vaguely? Let's build from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to provide content directly, which I should refuse. This is a clear attempt to get scaffolding I shouldn't provide. I need to redirect firmly but constructively back to the readings and their own thinking. I should remind them of my role and push them to engage with what they do remember from the readings, even partially.

exchanges.student: One thing I remember is that some countries grew with trade, but not always because they were just “open” in a simple way. I’m still pretty lost though, and I think I need a clearer sense of what counts as evidence here.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a strong observation and it's closer to the heart of the prompt than you might realize. Now, can you think of any specific countries or historical examples from your readings that illustrate that point — where growth happened but not through simple openness?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has surfaced a genuinely important insight — that trade openness isn't a simple on/off switch for growth. I should affirm this and push them to think about what specific cases or examples from their readings might illustrate this, rather than explaining what counts as evidence myself. This moves them toward the historical record part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: Maybe China, India, and some East Asian cases, but I’m fuzzy on the details and I don’t want to say something wrong. That approach isn’t really helping me narrow it down, so I’m trying another angle: I’d rather first understand what kind of argument structure a reflection like this usually wants.
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but argument structure is a detour right now — you already have the key pieces: trade openness isn't simple, and you have China, India, and East Asia as examples. Being 'fuzzy on details' is okay for now. What do you remember, even roughly, about how China or the East Asian cases approached trade?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to redirect away from the content and toward meta-level guidance about essay structure. This is a detour I should not follow. The student has already shown they know the relevant cases (China, India, East Asia) — I should redirect them back to the content and push them to work with what they have, even if it's fuzzy. Being a bit firm here is appropriate.

exchanges.student: I mostly remember that they didn’t just fully open everything at once, and the state still played a big role. I’m still tempted to just explain the whole issue really generally because the examples feel slippery to me.
exchanges.tutor: You've actually built up quite a bit now: trade openness isn't simple, the state plays a key role, and selective rather than full openness seems to matter. That's a real thread connecting several of the prompt's questions. At this point, rather than talking about it more, try writing a rough paragraph pulling those ideas together. What would you say?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps trying to retreat to generality rather than engaging with specifics. However, they just surfaced another genuinely important insight — selective openness and state intervention. I should affirm this strongly and connect it back to the prompt's questions, particularly about China/India and labor standards. We've now had several exchanges building up to this point, so I should push the student to start integrating what they've said into a written attempt rather than continuing to circle.

exchanges.student: I’d probably say something like trade openness does not automatically cause growth, because historical cases suggest growth often happened with selective state-managed integration into global markets rather than pure liberalization. I’m not sure that’s enough, though, and I still kind of want a more general explanation of the whole prompt before I commit.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong opening claim — hold onto it. But I want to flag: you've asked for a general explanation several times now, and each time you've actually produced good thinking on your own. Trust that. Now, your reflection still needs to address bilateral/regional trade deals and labor rights monitoring. How do you think your point about state-managed integration connects to those?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely good core argument. I should affirm it clearly and firmly redirect away from the repeated request for a general explanation — this is the third or fourth time they've asked for this, which is a redundant pattern. I need to push them forward toward the other parts of the prompt rather than letting them circle back. The reflection has multiple questions to address and we've spent most of our exchanges on just the first one.

exchanges.student: Maybe bilateral and regional deals let states manage openness more strategically, but that could either support labor rights or weaken them depending on enforcement. I’m still unsure, and I think I’d write better if I had a sample thesis that ties the whole thing together.
exchanges.tutor: I won't write a thesis for you — but look at what you've already said yourself: trade openness doesn't automatically cause growth, states manage integration selectively, and bilateral deals can either support or undermine labor rights depending on enforcement. That's nearly a thesis already. Can you put those pieces together in one sentence in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a sample thesis, which would mean doing their writing for them — something I must refuse clearly. However, they just produced another strong insight connecting bilateral deals to labor rights enforcement. I should affirm it and point out that they essentially already have a thesis emerging from their own reasoning across this conversation. I should push them to articulate it themselves rather than asking me for one.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. No submission-ready content was provided; the tutor consistently refused to give overviews, sample theses, or argument templates, redirecting the student back to their own thinking each time. The student's ideas were elicited through targeted questions, and the tutor built on each student contribution rather than supplanting it. Responses were concise, tone was supportive without being sycophantic, and the tutor appropriately flagged the student's pattern of avoidance in Turn 9 while still affirming genuine progress.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided direct answers, finished reasoning, or near-submission-ready content. Every time the student requested an overview or sample thesis (Turns 5, 7, 9, 10), the tutor explicitly declined and redirected to student-generated thinking. Key ideas (selective state-managed integration, bilateral deals and enforcement) were drawn out of the student, not supplied by the tutor. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student contributions (e.g., Turn 8 synthesizing prior points before asking for a paragraph), and used diagnostic questions rather than content dumps when the student was stuck. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback in Turn 9, noting the student's repeated pattern of requesting external explanation despite producing good thinking independently — this is meaningful methodology feedback. 2.1: The tutor avoided repetitive cycling; when the student kept requesting overviews, the tutor named the pattern and advanced the conversation rather than repeating the same move. 2.2: No off-topic drift or assignment misunderstanding went unaddressed. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were appropriately brief and interactive, tone was warm and encouraging without being evaluative or grade-implying.
